headline: A comet, a battle in Sussex, and a world half-oblivious to England's revolution
summary: In 1066 the Anglo-Saxon world ended at Hastings, and a French-speaking dynasty took over an island whose language and law it would rewrite for centuries. To contemporaries elsewhere the more visible portent was the return of Halley's Comet in April, watched from Constantinople, Kaifeng, and Cairo alike. Song China was in a phase of extraordinary technological ferment, Fatimid Egypt was sliding into famine and civil war under its longest-reigning caliph, the Seljuk sultanate was reshaping the Islamic east, and the Chola navy dominated the Bay of Bengal. Trans-Saharan gold poured from the empire of Ghana at Koumbi Saleh into a Muslim Mediterranean whose learned classes still barely noticed the western island where Duke William had just crowned himself king on Christmas Day.

snapshots.text: The year opened with the death of King Edward the Confessor on 5 January 1066 and the immediate coronation, on 6 January, of Harold Godwinson at the newly consecrated Westminster Abbey. Two rival claimants prepared to invade: Duke William of Normandy, backed by a papal banner from Pope Alexander II, and King Harald Hardrada of Norway. In late September Harold marched north and destroyed Harald Hardrada's army at Stamford Bridge outside York on 25 September, often called the last Viking invasion of England. Three days later William landed unopposed at Pevensey in Sussex. The battle on Senlac ridge near Hastings on 14 October 1066 lasted from dawn to dusk; Harold and his brothers Gyrth and Leofwine were killed and the English shield wall broke at nightfall. William was crowned in Westminster Abbey on Christmas Day. On the continent, Halley's Comet had blazed across the sky in April and was recorded as an omen by chroniclers from Bayeux to Byzantium (the Bayeux Tapestry, embroidered within two decades, depicts it above Harold's coronation). The Iberian Reconquista was quickening: Fernando I of León-Castile had died in December 1065, dividing his kingdoms among his sons. In Germany, the child-emperor Henry IV was still under regency, and in the Kievan Rus the aging Grand Prince Iziaslav I sat at Kiev. In late December, in the taifa of Granada, a Muslim mob killed the Jewish vizier Joseph ibn Naghrela and massacred much of the city's Jewish community — one of the earliest such pogroms in medieval Iberia.

In Cairo the Fatimid caliph al-Mustansir Billah, in the thirtieth year of what would become a sixty-year reign, presided over a court sliding toward catastrophe. The vizier al-Yazuri had been executed in 1058, and the subsequent decade saw a rapid succession of viziers as Turkish, Berber, and Sudanese army factions fought for control of Egypt. The following years, from about 1067, are remembered in Arab chronicles as the Mustansirite Hardship (al-Shidda al-Mustansiriyya), a seven-year famine caused by low Nile floods; parts of the Fatimid palace library, which held perhaps two hundred thousand volumes, were sold off or looted, its bindings reused. Al-Azhar mosque in Cairo, founded in 972, remained the intellectual heart of Isma'ili learning. Further east the Seljuk sultan Alp Arslan, having succeeded his uncle Tughril Beg in 1063, was campaigning in the Caucasus and Armenia; his crushing victory over the Byzantine emperor Romanos IV at Manzikert would come in 1071 and open Anatolia to Turkish settlement. In Baghdad the Abbasid caliph al-Qa'im reigned as spiritual figurehead under Seljuk protection; the Abu Hanifa Mosque was established there in 1066. Nasir Khusraw's earlier travel accounts of Cairo, Mecca, and the Levant remained the great literary window onto this eleventh-century Islamic world.

Song China under the short-lived Emperor Yingzong (r. 1063-1067) was probably the most technologically advanced and economically dynamic society on earth. The Northern Song capital Kaifeng, on the lower Yellow River, was almost certainly the largest city in the world, with paper money (jiaozi) circulating locally, night markets, guilds, and a population perhaps approaching one million. In 1066 the historian and future chancellor Sima Guang (1019-1086) presented to the throne the first section of what would become the Zizhi Tongjian, the vast chronological history of China from 403 BCE onward, produced under imperial patronage. Bi Sheng's invention of movable-type printing, roughly two decades earlier, had joined an already sophisticated woodblock printing industry that put Buddhist and Confucian texts within reach of a broad literate elite recruited by the imperial examination system. Yingzong's son Shenzong would ascend the following year and appoint Wang Anshi to launch the sweeping New Policies reforms of state finance, taxation, and militia service. To the north the Khitan Liao empire under Emperor Daozong still controlled the Sixteen Prefectures south of the Great Wall; to the northwest the Tangut Western Xia state was consolidating along the Silk Road. In Japan the aging Emperor Go-Reizei presided from Heian-kyō (Kyoto) while the Fujiwara regents wielded actual power; on the Korean peninsula the Goryeo dynasty under King Munjong was flourishing culturally, patronising Buddhist woodblock printing and refined celadon ceramics.

Southern India was dominated by the Chola empire at its imperial zenith. King Virarajendra Chola (r. 1063-1070) ruled from the great planned capital of Gangaikonda Cholapuram in the Kaveri delta, built earlier in the century by his predecessor Rajendra I to commemorate a victorious northern campaign to the Ganges. Chola naval power reached across the Bay of Bengal: the earlier expedition of 1025 against the Srivijayan thalassocracy of Sumatra was followed by further campaigns into the Malay peninsula, including the seizure of Kedah, and by dense diplomatic and commercial contact with the Song court. Chola bronze casting — the great dancing Shiva Nataraja icons — was in its classical phase, and the enormous Brihadeeswarar temple at Thanjavur, completed under Rajaraja I in 1010, still dominated the delta skyline. Northern India was a mosaic of Rajput kingdoms — Paramaras at Dhara, Chandelas at Khajuraho with its famous temples then being completed, Gahadavalas at Kannauj — that had recovered from Mahmud of Ghazni's raids of the early eleventh century. In eastern India the Buddhist Pala dynasty still ruled Bengal and Bihar, supporting the great monastic universities of Nalanda and Vikramashila. On the Central Asian steppe the Kipchak and Kimek confederations were dominant; further east the Kara-Khanid khanate, first Turkic Muslim dynasty, ruled Transoxiana and Kashgar.

In the western Sahel the empire of Ghana (Wagadu) under the ruler Tunka Manin, who had succeeded around 1063, was at the height of its power. The Andalusi geographer al-Bakri, writing in 1067-1068 from Córdoba on the basis of merchants' accounts, gave the classic description of the capital Koumbi Saleh on the desert edge of what is now southeastern Mauritania: a double city, with a Muslim quarter of stone and clay houses and twelve mosques serving traders, jurists, and imams, and a separate royal town where the king held audience surrounded by horses caparisoned in gold and by the sacred grove of his ancestral religion. Ghana taxed the trans-Saharan trade in gold coming north from the Bambuk and Bure fields and in salt going south from Taghaza. To the west and north, Abd Allah ibn Yasin's Almoravid movement — founded a generation earlier — had already conquered Sijilmasa and Awdaghust and was advancing across Morocco toward al-Andalus; Yusuf ibn Tashfin would found Marrakech in 1070. In the middle Nile, the Christian Nubian kingdoms of Makuria and Alwa continued a centuries-old accommodation with Fatimid Egypt. Around Lake Chad the Kanem kingdom under the Sayfawa dynasty was consolidating. Along the Swahili coast, port towns like Shanga, Manda, and Kilwa were building their first coral-stone mosques and connecting the East African interior to Indian Ocean trade in gold, ivory, and mangrove wood.

In central Mexico the Toltec state, whose capital Tollan (modern Tula in Hidalgo) was renowned in later Aztec memory for its warrior-columns and skilled artisans, was in its florescence under a lineage of rulers whose names — Ce Acatl Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl and his successors — survive only through much later semi-legendary sources. Toltec cultural influence extended as far as the northern Yucatán, where the Itzá of Chichen Itza had built or rebuilt monumental complexes such as the Temple of the Warriors and El Castillo pyramid in a hybrid Maya-Toltec style. Elsewhere in the Maya lowlands the great Classic centres such as Tikal and Palenque had been abandoned for two centuries, and political life had shifted north. In the American Southwest, Chaco Canyon in northwestern New Mexico was entering the great century of its 'Chaco Phenomenon': the Ancestral Puebloan multi-storey great house of Pueblo Bonito, along with Chetro Ketl and Pueblo del Arroyo, was being expanded into hundreds of rooms and dozens of kivas, connected by hundreds of miles of engineered roads to outlier communities across the San Juan basin. In the Mississippi valley Cahokia, just east of modern St Louis, was rising toward the peak it would reach around 1100 as the largest urban settlement north of Mexico, its Monks Mound already being raised in stages. In the central Andes the Wari state had collapsed and the Chimu at Chan Chan on the north Peruvian coast were building the largest adobe city in the pre-Columbian world.

The Pacific in the eleventh century was in the middle of the greatest single expansion of the Polynesian voyaging tradition. According to recent genomic and archaeological work, ancestral Polynesians from Samoa and Tonga were reaching the Society Islands around Tahiti in the eleventh century, and would push out from there over the following two centuries to the Marquesas, the Tuamotus, the Australs, and eventually — around 1200 — to remote Rapa Nui (Easter Island), and — by the late thirteenth and early fourteenth centuries — to Hawaii and Aotearoa (New Zealand). On these islands, marae ceremonial platforms of coral-slab and basalt were being built and the political systems of chiefly lineages and priestly experts were taking shape. In Australia, hundreds of Aboriginal nations continued centuries-old traditions of land management, ceremony, and exchange; specific dated events for 1066 do not survive because these were oral societies, but the archaeological record documents continuing occupation of every biome from the northern rainforests to the arid centre and Tasmania. In New Guinea, highland farmers cultivated taro, sweet potato (introduced from South America around this era through as-yet-debated pathways), sugar cane, and banana in intensively drained valley systems; on the coasts, Austronesian-speaking peoples maintained the trade networks that would centuries later form the Kula and Hiri systems of the Massim and Papuan Gulf.
